_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Morgan County, IN. Record survival for the county centered on Martinsville. Partial loss from early courthouse fire.

previous_courthouses.fate: Log structure. Replaced by brick building.
Brick courthouse. Fire in 1855 damaged some records. Current Italianate courthouse built shortly after.

record_losses.description: A fire at the Morgan County courthouse in 1855 damaged some early records. The most critical bound volumes — deed books and will books — largely survived because they were stored in a vault. Loose papers, some court minutes, and scattered early documents were lost. The damage was not catastrophic but created gaps in the earliest decades of the county's records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} had business in the Morgan County courthouse before 1855, some records may have been lost in the fire. Deed books and will books largely survived, but loose court papers from the 1820s-1840s are fragmentary. The fire makes early Morgan County research harder than in neighboring counties.
record_losses.source: Blanchard, Charles, ed. Counties of Morgan, Monroe, and Brown, Indiana. Chicago: F.A. Battey, 1884.

deeds.completeness: Good — most deed books survived the 1855 fire
deeds.gaps: Some early entries may be missing or damaged.
deeds.repository: Morgan County Recorder; Indiana State Archives

wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from earliest decades fragmentary due to fire damage.
wills_estates.repository: Morgan County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

marriages.repository: Morgan County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Fair — some early court minutes lost in the 1855 fire
court_minutes.gaps: Gaps in the 1822-1855 period. Post-fire records complete.
court_minutes.repository: Morgan County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for supplementing records lost in the 1855 fire. Census records from 1830, 1840, and 1850 may be the only documentation for early Morgan County families whose court records were destroyed.

substitute_sources.source_type: Indiana land records — federal land office
substitute_sources.description: Original land patents from the federal government predate county deed records and are held at the Indiana State Archives and Bureau of Land Management.
substitute_sources.repository: Bureau of Land Management General Land Office records; Indiana State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Federal land patents document the first purchase of land from the government — these records survive regardless of county courthouse fires.
substitute_sources.source: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records.

narrative_hooks: The 1855 courthouse fire in Martinsville created gaps in Morgan County's earliest records — if {ancestor_name}'s court business predates the fire, the documentation may be gone.
Deed books largely survived the fire because they were in the vault — {ancestor_name}'s land records probably exist even if other court records from the same era were lost.

For early Morgan County families, federal land patents at the Indiana State Archives supplement what the courthouse fire destroyed — the original purchase from the government survives even when the county copy does not.
Before 1882, a missing vital record for {ancestor_name} in Morgan County is standard for all Indiana counties — the fire is not the reason there is no birth certificate.
source_references: Indiana Commission on Public Records. Guide to Indiana County Records.
Blanchard, Charles, ed. Counties of Morgan, Monroe, and Brown, Indiana. Chicago: F.A. Battey, 1884.
Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records.
